summary: Updated the `tools/frank.py` binary post-processing tool by merging changes from `projectPiki/pikmin#5`. The change replaced a simple removal of the profiling epilogue byte sequence with a peephole rescheduling pass that detects specific instruction reorderings around inserted `bl`/`nop` profiling bytes and restores vanilla instruction ordering before stripping those extra bytes.

key_files.role: updated binary transformation tool; added profile extra byte constant and peephole instruction rescheduling before stripping profiling bytes

what_changed: Added `PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES` as a named constant for the byte sequence `48 00 00 01 60 00 00 00`, replacing the previous inline literal used by `replace`.
Inserted a peephole rescheduling loop that scans `profile_bytes` for the profiling byte sequence on 4-byte-aligned boundaries.
Tracked `shift`, the accumulated size difference between vanilla and profiled code due to inserted profiling bytes, to map profile offsets back to vanilla offsets.
Compared nearby vanilla and profiled instructions around each profiling sequence to detect schedule swaps involving `lwz`, `li`/`lfs`, and unchanged following instructions.
When a recognized swap is detected, rewrote `profile_bytes` to use the vanilla instruction order around the profiling bytes.
Added a second special case for `lwz`/`lmw` reordering where both instructions follow the inserted profiling bytes.
Changed final stripping to remove `PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES` after any detected rescheduling has been corrected.
smart_moves: Named the profiling byte sequence as `PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES`, making the later scanning and stripping logic less error-prone than duplicating a raw byte literal.
Performed peephole correction before removing the extra profiling bytes, preserving enough positional context to identify schedule differences around the inserted `bl`/`nop` sequence.
Used opcode-level checks derived from the first instruction byte shifted by two, avoiding the need for a full PowerPC disassembler while still recognizing relevant instruction classes.
Required exact byte equality between paired vanilla/profile instructions before rewriting, which limits the transformation to strongly evidenced reorderings.
Included a 4-byte-alignment check before treating a byte sequence as a profiling marker, reducing the risk of acting on accidental byte matches.
Maintained a `shift` counter so vanilla/profile offsets remain comparable as each profiling byte sequence accounts for an 8-byte difference.
Avoided reordering at the apparent end of an epilogue by excluding cases where the following opcode is `ADDI`.
decomp_lessons: For decompilation support tools that compare vanilla and instrumented binaries, removing injected bytes may not be enough; inserted calls/nops can perturb local instruction scheduling and require targeted normalization.
Peephole binary rewriting can be sufficient for narrow compiler-scheduling artifacts when the transformation is guarded by exact byte comparisons against the vanilla binary.
When handling profiled or otherwise modified binaries, explicitly model the byte delta introduced by instrumentation so offsets can be mapped back to the original binary.
Small, local binary normalization passes should run before destructive stripping steps if the inserted bytes help identify the affected region.
A narrowly scoped opcode classifier can be an effective compromise between raw byte replacement and full instruction decoding for matching tools.
naming_conventions: `PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES` follows the existing uppercase constant style used by `CODESIZE_MAGIC`, `BLR_BYTE_SEQ`, and `MTLR_BYTE_SEQ`.
Local variables use descriptive snake_case names such as `profile_bytes`, `stripped_bytes`, `epi_pos`, `v_pos`, `vanilla_inst_a`, and `profile_inst_a`.
Temporary instruction labels `vanilla_inst_a`, `vanilla_inst_b`, and `vanilla_inst_c` mirror the explanatory comment's `(A)`, `(B)`, and following-instruction notation.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Detect the inserted profiling epilogue marker with `profile_bytes.find(PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES, idx)` and require `epi_pos % 4 == 0` for instruction alignment.
Map profile positions to vanilla positions using `v_pos = epi_pos - shift`, then increment `shift` by 8 for each profiling byte sequence.
Extract 4-byte PowerPC instructions around the marker from both vanilla and profiled byte arrays and compare them directly.
Classify opcodes from the first instruction byte using `inst[0] >> 2`; constants include `LWZ`, `LFS`, `ADDI`, `LI`, and `LMW`.
Recognize a schedule swap where vanilla has `lwz` then `li`/`lfs`, while the profiled code has those two instructions swapped around the inserted profiling bytes.
Restore vanilla ordering by splicing `profile_bytes` so the `lwz` precedes `PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES` and the `li`/`lfs` follows it.
Handle a similar `lwz`/`lmw` reorder case where both affected instructions appear after the inserted profiling bytes.
Only after peephole correction, strip all occurrences of `PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES` from `profile_bytes`.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify `tools/frank.py` peephole rescheduling or `PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES` handling.
Search for references to `projectPiki/pikmin#5` to understand the upstream source of the matching workaround.
Look for PRs discussing profiled binary stripping, `bl`/`nop` insertion, or instruction schedule normalization.
Check whether later tooling replaced this raw-byte peephole logic with a more general PowerPC instruction decoder.
